data.title: Churchill Downs: Strong Buy Driven By Margin Expansion And Capital Discipline
data.description:
Margin Momentum Unlocked

We initiate on Churchill Downs Incorporated (NASDAQ:CHDN) at Strong Buy with a $131 PT. CHDN is a leading U.S. racing, online wagering, and gaming operator across three fast-growing segments: Live and Historical Racing, TwinSpires, and Gaming. Our Strong Buy is based on three conviction drivers:

1) We model $6.45 and $7.30 of diluted EPS for FY25E and FY26E (13% annualized and meaningfully ahead of consensus), driven by the company's $900mn Skye/Conservatory hold, which materially reduces capital charges and unlocks >$0.30 of incremental annual EPS by FY26E.

2) Management is accelerating high-margin HRM expansion in VA, with ramping The Rose and additional Richmond/Henrico capacity driving a 37% machine count increase at 45-50% incremental EBITDA margins.

3) The $500mn share repurchase will retire 4-5% of shares in 18 months, compounding a strong per-share profile. Market focus on transitory Derby ticket softness/1Q headwinds misses the improving core profitability at CHDN, and trading at the 32nd historical percentile for P/E, we see our 18.0x 2026 forward target multiple as well-supported.

Key risks include market caution on sector multiples/near-term event volatility, which we see as largely manageable vs. the upside. Bottom line: our high conviction is driven by a unique combination of above-consensus earnings growth, margin expansion, and capital returns, which we see as attractive from a R/R perspective for forward-looking investors.

Margin Expansion Unlocked By Capital Discipline

For sophisticated investors in search of underappreciated catalysts, Churchill Downs Incorporated’s capital allocation pivot is nothing less than transformational. The opportunity is massive: by strategically pausing the high-cost, multi-year Skye / Conservatory / Infield projects at Churchill Downs Racetrack (originally budgeting $900mn), management has irrevocably sharpened the company’s margin trajectory at the exact same moment when consensus remains laser-focused on event-driven volatility. Here’s the capital allocation trail: CHDN has reduced its 2025 project capex forecast by a remarkable $100–110mn, with total 2025 project spend now guided at a mere $250–290mn and maintenance capital reduced by $10mn to $90–100mn.

The financial impact is immediate, dramatic, and real: 1Q free cash flow jumped to $234mn, or $3.15 per share, providing the dry powder for aggressive buybacks and further leverage paydown. This deliberate capital pullback is not an exercise in waiting out macro uncertainty — it is sheer capital discipline, allowing the company to sidestep cost inflation risk while prioritizing smaller, higher-return investments such as the $25–30mn Finish Line Suites and Mansion upgrades, which are on track to deliver robust, low-risk shareholder value.

Virginia HRM buildout, the margin driver that underpins Churchill's next leg up

The opportunity comes into focus when isolating CHDN's hyper-focused rolling out of high-margin HRMs in Virginia, a move that flips the EBITDA comp equation. In '25, The Rose Gaming Resort opened with 1,650 HRMs + WPD grew 20% MoM through Feb, highlighting strong latent demand in the 3rd largest domestic gaming market (ex. Vegas). Not a one-and-done, Churchill's HRM penetration is scaling: VA installed base increased from 2.3k at acquisition to 4.45k in mid-'25, with a clear glidepath to the legal cap of 5.0k before YE (via imminent expansions in Richmond/Henrico). This is a transformative EBITDA lever: VA HRM venues generated $129.3mn in Q1 '25 revenues (+16% y/y) and contributed ~24% of total company EBITDA generation annually (launch drag from upfront marketing + weather volatility).

The kicker: new HRM units are modeled at 45-50% incremental EBITDA margins, and analysts see the segment as contributing a game-changing 41% of total property-level EBITDA in '26E (as HRM EBITDA grows 60% from '23 to '26E). The flywheel is real: as regional gaming faces cyclical headwinds and Derby volatility persists, the recurring, high-margin HRM cash flows turbocharge FCF/delevering. Bottom line: The VA HRM buildout is not just accretive growth — it is a transformational margin engine and structural cash flow driver that the market is missing, setting up for significant upside for the patient investor.

CHDN Above-Cons EPS Trajectory on HRM Leverage, Buyback Acceleration, Sharply Lower Cap Charges

We estimate FY25E/FY26E diluted EPS of $6.45/$7.30, vs. the street's $6.08/$6.73, reflecting 13% annualized EPS growth (vs. ~7-11% consensus) — a disconnect that, we believe, is meaningfully underappreciated by the market. We are driving this above consensus trajectory on

(1) strategic pause on $900mn Skye/Conservatory project, driving outsized interest and depreciation reduction, unlocking +$0.30+ of annual EPS by '26;

(2) substantial high-margin HRM capacity acceleration across VA, driven by the Rose's improving performance and capacity additions at Richmond and Henrico, driving +37% machine count mat and 45-50% incremental EBITDA margins; and

(3) aggressive $500mn share buyback program in place at depressed valuation levels, driving 4-5% share count reduction within 12–18 months of the program.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $2.91B 9 $3.04B 10
EBITDA $1.04B 9 $1.09B 10
Net Income $453.26M 9 $506.67M 10
EPS $6.08 7 $6.73 5
SG&A Expenses $264.90M 9 $276.76M 10

We believe the street is fixated on softness in Q1 and ticket demand at Derby, though we see a transitory weakness. Derby-151 is running in-line and the weather is normalizing from Q2. Yes, we concede that the street could remain unimpressed on the upside if it remains focused on sector multiples, but we are above the street on fundamentals in FY25E and FY26E.

Valuation

Our $131 PT is set by applying an 18.0x FY26E P/E multiple to our above-consensus FY26E EPS of $7.30 (42% upside). The target multiple is defensible given CHDN's estimated 13.2% EPS CAGR (vs peer/consensus), enabled by the Skye/Conservatory pause ($900mn) and Virginia's accelerated and high margin HRMs (both material capital charge relief) with the $500mn buyback providing incremental EPS leverage and substantial confidence in the intrinsic value with management's strong involvement.

Our target multiple is conservative considering the 21.2x historical median (32nd pctile) and a large discount to the historical 87% premium vs peers when our forward P/E is only modestly above the depressed peer median of 13.2x. Critically, while the market is fixated on transient Derby softness, the overlooked catalyst by consensus, in our view, is structurally leveraged margins from capital discipline compounding through the cycle. We view sector multiple caution and transitory headwinds over-discounted and CHDN's re-rating underappreciated.

Risks to Our Strong Buy thesis

Despite high conviction in unique margin expansion and capital return prospects, our CHDN thesis would be undermined by a tougher-than-expected macro or regulatory environment compounding through the remainder of the year, resulting in an enduring decline in regional gaming demand—particularly among the unrated/low-end market segment (a negative trend management just highlighted following Q1 results and the subsequent decline in the share price).

Similarly, current outsized capital discipline appears largely dependent on the suspension of the $900mn Skye/Conservatory project, as ongoing concerns over real or perceived trade wars, inflationary pressures, and tariff-inflated labor & materials costs on construction projects create an increasingly longer trail. The resulting erosion of long-term Kentucky Derby and hospitality earnings ramp would necessitate a strategic reevaluation of capital allocation priorities in light of outsized growth sensitivity.

Finally, CHDN's ongoing expansion of HRM and online wagering remains the centerpiece of our bullish thesis, but any negative regulatory developments (taxation, licensing, legal, etc.) could constrain margin leverage/growth potential, particularly in light of recent regulatory headwinds in MI for TwinSpires. This includes potential developments in VA or KY, or headline controversies.

We outline our key risk scenarios in context of the aforementioned outlined investment thesis above, and highlight the following fundamental data points to gauge the trajectory of risk events to inform valuation: 1) Monthly gaming/HRM revenue trends; 2) Derby-week revenue results; 3) State legislative actions impacting HRMs and online wagering; and 4) Real-time operating cost pressures.

Conclusion

We believe that Churchill Downs offers a compelling opportunity at current levels as margin momentum and capital discipline catalyze a sustained rerate that is underappreciated relative to the market's near-term focus on Derby volatility. With the Skye/Conservatory pause unlocking incremental EPS and management accelerating high-margin HRM expansion in Virginia, we see the core earnings trajectory and capital return profile as significantly underappreciated. For forward-looking investors, we view the current discounted entry point as a compelling risk/reward to build positions ahead of consensus as it incorporates structural higher profitability.

data.title: ILF: Valuation Meets Momentum, Upgrading To Buy
data.description:
It’s hard to be too bearish on global stocks when most ex-US markets are hitting all-time highs. That’s the situation right now, and readers know that I have been upbeat on foreign equities, but there has been a surprising development south of the border.

The iShares Latin America 40 ETF (NYSEARCA:ILF) is up 23% so far this year, dividends included. That’s 20 percentage points of alpha to the S&P 500. Most of the major “Americas” country ETFs have returned more than 20% in 2025, and I assert more upside is ahead.

I am upgrading ILF from a hold to a buy. I was neutral on the fund in Q3 last year, noting weak relative strength despite an attractive valuation. Today, it still has a low P/E, but its outperformance metrics are outstanding, which warrants the upgrade.

Global Stock Markets Soaring in 2025

ILF Beating SPY By 20ppt YTD

According to the issuer, ILF focuses on investing in the public equity markets of Latin America and the Caribbean. The fund targets growth and value stocks of large-cap companies across various sectors, and it aims to track the performance of the S&P Latin America 40 index, which consists of the 40 largest Latin American stocks.

ILF is a small ETF with $1.7 billion in assets under management as of June 4, 2025. That’s up from $1.4 billion at the time of my September 2024 analysis. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 48 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is very high at 6.1%. Such a high distribution sum leads to a somewhat distorted technical view, but I will point out a key bullish pattern I see developing (later in the article).

As it stands, ILF’s share-price momentum ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system has undergone a bullish reversal—from an F grade just three months ago to a respectable B-. Still, the fund is highly risky, primarily due to its concentrated and extreme level of portfolio cyclicality. Finally, liquidity is solid, given average daily volume of more than 1.7 million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of only four basis points, per iShares.

For a refresh on the portfolio, the 4-star ETF by Morningstar plots along the top row of the style box, indicating its bent toward large caps. So, you won’t find a whole lot of small or medium-sized companies in the fund, which does help to limit risk.

But with high exposure to economically dependent industries, the low price-to-earnings ratio of 8.5x makes sense. The long-term EPS growth rate is not all that high at 7.6%, but the resulting PEG ratio is attractive at just 1.12x.

ILF: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

What makes ILF volatile is that there’s a more than 50% exposure to Financials and Materials—two cyclical areas of the stock market. Interest rate changes have been up and down lately, which can make planning for banks difficult. Such uncertainty can also crimp capital markets activity. Moreover, while commodities like copper have been quite impressive so far in the year, industrial metals, too, can swing around. So, there are risks to weigh.

In all, the top 10 stocks comprise 54% of the fund, which is the concentrated side.

ILF: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal point of view, June tends to be a decent month, but July has historically been the best month for Latin American stocks. Hence, we are in the throes of a solid calendar stretch, but be on guard for potential volatility in August and September.

ILF: Bullish Seasonal Trends Through Early Q3

The Technical Take

With a compelling valuation and sublime YTD relative strength, ILF’s technical view is encouraging. Notice in the chart below that shares have recently approached key resistance between $26 and $27—if it breaks out above that range, then a measured move upside price objective to about $33 is in play based on the height of the head and shoulders reversal pattern that goes back to the time of my previous assessment.

Also, take a look at where ILF bottomed in January and April—there was a double-bottom reversal pattern, which augurs for higher prices ahead. Also, the RSI momentum oscillator made a higher low at the April nadir, and the long-term 200-day moving average may soon inflect positive—still work for the bulls to do there.

To be clear, the total return chart is much better looking, and the 6%+ yield makes chart analysis less useful (though it’s still important to gauge).

ILF: Key Resistance Between $26 and $27, $33 Upside Target, Solid RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on ILF. I see the Latin America 40 ETF as sporting a still-low valuation, while both absolute and relative strength have improved sharply from my September analysis.

data.title: XCCC: Great Fund, Poor Macro
data.description:
Thesis

We first started covering the BondBloxx CCC Rated USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:XCCC) last year here, where we articulated why we found the exchange-traded fund to be a great addition to the fixed income universe by allowing investors to focus on a particular rating band from the high yield (i.e. junk) space. We love what BondBloxx did here by creating the ETF, and thus allowing retail investors to access this niche part of the junk universe.

Hopefully, the name can grow in AUM and institutions can use it as a proxy for CCC names the same way they use iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) and SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) as proxies for large, diversified baskets of high yielding securities.

In today's article, we are going to have a look at the fund's latest performance and composition, and articulate why it represents a 'Hold' for aggressive investors given the macro context.

A diversified CCC fund

There should be no questions on what the name contains given its objective and index benchmark:

BondBloxx CCC Rated USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF seeks to track the investment results of the ICE CCC US Cash Pay High Yield Constrained Index which contains all bonds in the ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index that are rated CCC1 through CCC3, based on an average of Moody’s Investors Services, Inc., S&P Global Ratings and Fitch Ratings, Inc, but caps issuer exposure at 2%.

Please note that diversification is achieved via issuer capping at 2%, which ensures that even as names default, the fund is able to take the hits in stride. Issuer capping is better than debenture capping, since an issuer can have several bonds outstanding, and thus an issuer capping aggregates all relevant exposures.

The current names in the portfolio are as follows:

Please note that until the rebalancing date, certain names can have higher percentage weightings due to price gains. Upon rebalancing the weighting will go back to 2%.

The fund's credit rating profile is as follows:

  • Caa1: 46.2%
  • Caa2: 37.3%
  • Caa3: 14.7%

Although the fund does disclose that parsing, we are a bit less interested in the granularities here. All CCC names are highly risky, and what actually matters here is Loss Given Default ('LGD'). If a name has a small loss upon default (think a very asset rich company) then we should care less about the probability of default. If the loss is almost total (think software companies) then a default means an almost complete wipe-out for the fund for that issuer balance.

Unfortunately, LGDs are historically high as per this S&P paper:

Remember, a high loss rate simply means the bond will get almost wiped-out during a default, thus creating a true loss for the ETF. During recessions losses tend to get exacerbated due to a high cost of capital to move forward with a restructuring.

Other portfolio details are as follows:

There are currently 216 securities in the portfolio, coming from only 137 issuers. The average maturity for the held paper is 3.9 years, and the option adjusted spread is 784 bps.

State of CCC spreads

When analyzing CCC heavy names, we like to look at CCC spreads, namely the ICE BofA CCC & Lower US High Yield Index:

Outside truly distressed environments like 2020, we see a clear 7.5% to 12.5% range. Just looking at the graph without knowing much, one can see why spreads would be bought above 10% and sold when close to the bottom of the range.

Make no mistake about it, moves here can be very violent and sudden. Suffice to look at what happened in April 2025, when spreads widened from 7.5% to close to 11% in a matter of weeks. The price impact for XCCC was just as abrupt:

The name recorded a sudden -12% drawdown, very much in-line with outright equity. Kindly keep in mind that CCC names represent debentures from fairly distressed companies, and the low rating implies a high probability of default.

Fund yields and analytics

Make no mistake about it - while the fund is very risky, it also rewards a holder via a juicy 12.5% 30-day SEC yield. Other interesting analytics are:

  • AUM: $0.14 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.4%
  • Yield: 12.5%
  • Payment period: monthly
  • Annualized volatility: 9.8%

The fund gets you paid, while having an acceptable drawdown during normalized market sell-offs.

Risk factors

The biggest risk factor here is a recession. While an investor can expect a -10% drawdown during normal market pullbacks, a recession means something else completely. If we look at historic spreads, we can see that CCCs had 20% spreads during Covid. Will that repeat itself? We think not. However, we do think that a recession can see 14% spreads. Especially a prolonged recession. That spread level can translate into a -20% drawdown and potential outright losses in the portfolio via defaults.

A quick 'v' shaped recession is the best one to have, whereas a long, painful one which would see a high number of defaults would be the worst-case scenario here. CCC bonds are the first to default, usually because the issuing companies are already in distress.

Conclusion

We like XCCC very much as a fixed income building block. The ETF focuses solely on CCC names, and does a good job at building a granular diversified portfolio. The fund has a high 12.5% yield which is paid monthly, and one can expect a -10% drawdown during normalized market risk-off events. The largest risk factor here is a long recession that can see outright losses hit the fund. Given we are no longer at the bottom of the range in terms of spreads, but actually close to 10%, we are on 'Hold' with respect to XCCC.

data.title: The Gabelli Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
PORTFOLIO OBSERVATIONS

The Gabelli Growth Fund (MUTF:GABGX) returned (8.5)% during the first quarter, compared with a (10.0)% return for the Russell 1000 Growth Index and a (4.3)% return for the S&P 500.

During the quarter, we added to an existing position in Broadcom Inc. (AVGO, )2.5% of portfolio assets as of March 31, 2025). We did not initiate any new positions during the quarter.

Our largest position decreases in the quarter were CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD, 1.1%), Carrier Global (CARR, 0.8%), and Netflix (NFLX, 4.4%). We eliminated one holding during 1Q in Adobe Inc. (ADBE), which we believe is facing intensifying business model disruption from Generative AI.

For the first quarter, our top ten contributors to performance (based upon price change and position size) were GE Aerospace (GE, 1.3%), Spotify Technology (SPOT, 1.7%), Visa (V, 3.3%), O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY, 1.3%), Boston Scientific (BSX, 2.4%), Netflix (4.2%), Eli Lilly and Company (LLY, 3.8%), Mastercard (MA, 3.1%), Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG, 0.5%), and CrowdStrike Holdings (1.1%). On the flipside, the largest detractors from performance for the quarter were Nvidia Corp. (NVDA, 10.0%), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG,GOOGL, 4.3%), Microsoft (MSFT, 11.8%), Amazon.com (AMZN, 8.1%), and Broadcom (2.4%).

At a sector level, we ended the quarter with overweight exposures in Industrials (14.5% of portfolio assets compared with 8.5% in the Russell 1000 Growth) and Healthcare (10% of portfolio assets compared with 7.5% in the benchmark). Our largest sector underweights at quarter- end included Technology (52% of portfolio assets vs. 56% in the benchmark) and Consumer Staples (0% of portfolio assets vs. 2.5% in the benchmark).

Our Fund remains fairly concentrated, with positions in 33 companies at the end of the quarter. The top five holdings represent 45% of portfolio assets and our top ten holdings represent 66% of portfolio assets. We act like long-term owners of businesses in our portfolio and seek to maximize exposure to our best ideas. As a result of this approach, we occasionally expect above average price volatility over shorter time periods, though we believe this is the optimal way to create value over the long term.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Microsoft Corp. 11.8%
NVIDIA Corp. 10.0
Amazon.com Inc. 8.1
Apple Inc. 6.1
Meta Platforms Inc. 5.3
Alphabet Inc. 4.3
Netflix Inc. 4.2
Eli Lilly and Co. 3.8
Visa Inc. 3.3
Mastercard Inc. 3.1

*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

O'Reilly Automotive Inc. (1.4% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (ORLY - $1,432.58 - NASDAQ) has emerged as a high- quality operator gaining share in the consolidating U.S. aftermarket auto parts space. This is an attractive “defensive growth” category benefiting from secular growth trends, including an aging car fleet, growing car parc, and increasing vehicle complexity. Management estimates more than 80% of sales are attributable to non-discretionary and time-sensitive on-road failures, leading to strong pricing power. The space has also historically exhibited some counter-cyclical dynamics, as many consumers prefer investing in existing vehicles to purchasing new vehicles. Over the last several years, O'Reilly has shown particular momentum within the relatively faster-growing and stickier professional channel, given the company's expertise in inventory management and thoughtful geographic footprint strategy. Looking forward, we expect consistent revenue growth and margin expansion, with attractive capital allocation policies further supporting premium EPS growth.

Intuit Inc. (0.9%) (INTU - $613.99 - NASDAQ) is a financial software provider serving small businesses and consumers globally. The company operates a collection of franchises including QuickBooks (accounting and back-office software powering the day-to-day operations of small businesses), TurboTax (tax return software for consumers and accountants), and Credit Karma (credit scoring and monitoring software for consumers). Over the last several years, the company has been building out the QuickBooks product suite to appeal to larger merchants. This strategy has entailed the launch of new modules across payments, marketing services, payroll, and capital services, which have strengthened the company's positioning in the mid- market while also unlocking incremental monetization opportunities with existing customers. Most recently, Intuit has rolled out an AI Assistant called Intuit Assist, which customers across all banners are turning to for productivity enhancements and operational recommendations. With a captive customer base, an comprehensive and expanding product suite, and a coherent AI strategy, we see significant growth runway ahead.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS: Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A)

Gabelli Growth Fund QTR 1 Year 5 Year 10 Year 15 Year Since Inception (04/10/87)
Class I (MUTF:GGCIX) (B) (8.45)% 8.08% 16.31% 13.57% 13.41% 11.05%
S&P 500 Index (C) (4.27) 8.25 18.59 12.50 13.15 10.43
Russell 1000 Growth Index (C) (9.97) 7.76 20.09 15.12 15.29 10.73

Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

(A) Other classes of shares are available, with different characteristics. For additional information please contact your financial advisor or call 800-GABELLI.

(B) The Class AAA Share NAVs are used to calculate performance for the period prior to the issuance of Class I Shares on January 11, 2008. The actual performance of the Class I Shares would have been higher due to lower expenses related to this class of shares. The Fund imposes a 2% redemption fee on shares sold or exchanged within seven days of purchase.

(C) The S&P 500 Index is a market capitalization weighted index of 500 large capitalization stocks commonly used to represent the U.S. equity market. The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of the large cap growth segment of the U.S. equity market. You cannot invest directly in an index. Dividends are considered reinvested. Since inception performance is as of March 31, 1987.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate. When shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Visit Home for performance information as of the most recent month end.

Howard F. Ward, CFA, John T. Belton, CFA

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Gabelli Dividend Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT SCORECARD

The first quarter of 2025 was modestly positive for large cap value equities, even as the aggregate U.S. stock market began to sell off on concerns about tariffs and decelerating Al spend. Clearly, tariff levels announced on April 2nd were beyond almost any expectations, leading to broad-based declines in equity markets subsequent to quarter end. The reaction of equity and fixed income markets has forced a 90-day pause in the most extreme tariff levels, excepting for China (so far). Looking back, 2025 started off with a favorable thaw post-holiday, as extended holiday shutdowns came to an end and the consumer responded to favorable weather. February and March saw elevated interest rates ease, aided by tame inflation readings and an administration focused on lowering rates. The 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yield finished the quarter at 4.23%, down 35bps, while the February CPI eased to 2.8% y/y, benefiting defensive, dividend paying stocks.

The quarter also introduced negative crosscurrents. Threats of 25% tariffs against Mexico and Canada, which were ultimately postponed, created market angst. U.S. technology stock dominance was threatened by the success of China's DEEPSEEK, a large language model (LLM) trained by a hedge fund for less than $10 million. DeepSeek's ability to achieve similar LLM functionality with dramatically lower power and chip needs called into question trillions of Al-related planned CapEx. Accordingly, Nvidia (NVDA) shares finished the first quarter down 19%, and the stock of data center pure play Vertiv (VRT) down 36%. Despite early signs of economic slowing, the Fed suggested it would stay on hold for some time given the unpredictable impact of soon to be announced tariffs.

The S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) was down 4.3% for the first quarter of 2025. Growth stocks, as measured by the Russell 1000 Growth Index, were down 10.0%, led by a 12.7% decline in the S&P technology sector. Conversely, value stocks, as measured by the Russell 1000 Value Index, were up 2.1%, reflecting the benefit of lower interest rates for financial stocks and defensive sectors. In technology, concern about Al capex, competition, and monetization spilled over to the Mag 7, with cloud leaders Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN, 2.7% of net assets as of March 31, 2025), and Google (GOOG,GOOGL, 1.9%) down 11%, 13%, and 18%, respectively. Apple (AAPL, 0.8%) shares initially held up as investors embraced Apple Intelligence Al use cases, but then sold off as functionality and timelines for Apple Intelligence disappointed.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Merck & Co. Inc. (MRK) 3.7%
Citigroup Inc. (C) 3.5
Mondelēz International Inc. (MDLZ) 3.3
Kroger Co. (KR) 3.3
JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) 3.1
Visa Inc. (V) 2.9
Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) 2.7
Newmont Corp. (NEM) 2.6
PNC Financial Services Group Inc. (PNC) 2.4
Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) 2.3
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

The S&P financial sector, of greater relevance to the Fund, was up a solid 3.4% in the first quarter of 2025. Lower interest rates were deemed beneficial for the economy and credit, especially as the 2-10 Year U.S. Treasury spread maintained a healthy 30-35bps level. Optimism around deal activity and deregulation also helped financials. Defensive sectors all finished in the green, with the S&P healthcare sector up 6.5%, utilities up 4.9%, and staples up 5.0%. Industrials were flat, as tariff concerns offset improvement in the ISM manufacturing index.

For the first quarter ended March 31, 2025, (y)our portfolio was up 1.53%, while the broad market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index, was down 4.3%. The index mostly kept pace with the Russell 1000 Value Index, with the delta reflecting modest Mag7 exposure, namely Amazon, Alphabet, and Apple, with Apple mostly exited during the first quarter. The Fund continues to focus on owning solid businesses with leading or improving competitive positions and which trade at compelling valuations, inclusive of some Mag7 stocks.

The top contributors were Newmont (2.6%), T-Mobile (TMUS, 1.5%), and Mondelēz (3.3%). Newmont benefited from the strength in gold and gold equities more broadly as the dollar started to weaken and fear began to creep in the market. T-Mobile reported excellent subscriber growth and free cash flow for the fourth quarter and guided strong growth for 2025. The Fund more than halved its position in T-Mobile on valuation and more formidable AT&T competition. Mondelēz participated in a rebound in staples stocks, while cocoa prices, a key input, eased from high levels. It continues to execute well and is a beneficiary of a weaker dollar due to its high international exposure.

The biggest detractors were Amazon (2.7%), Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE, 1.3%), and Alphabet (1.9%). Amazon experienced headwinds from tariff uncertainty, a potential deceleration in Al and cloud investment, and weakening consumer confidence, although it is demonstrating solid retail margin improvement. Hewlett-Packard Enterprises has struggled from price/cost pressure in traditional servers, early tariff headwinds, a slower than expected ramp in Al server revenue, and the justice department suing to block its $14 billion acquisition of Juniper Networks. For Alphabet, concerns about Al search cannibalization remains, while cloud growth has been a bit lackluster given constraints on underlying infrastructure.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Zimmer Biomet (2.2%) (ZBH - $113.18 - NYSE) is the largest manufacturer and distribution of knee and hip implant solutions in the world. The demand for orthopedic procedures remains healthy due to aging and increasing active population, technological advancements, improving patient care and outcomes, and shift to outpatient ASC settings. Zimmer expects to launch 50 new products over the next three years to fill out its product portfolio in its various segments. In February 2025, Zimmer entered into an agreement to acquire Paragon 28 for $1.2 billion to enter into the foot and ankle market, which is growing faster than its company average and leverage its scale and further penetrate the ASC market. The company is growing its sales in line with market growth, but continues to be negatively impacted by foreign currency. With a healthy balance sheet, management remains strategic with its M&A to boost its organic sales growth.

PNC Financial Services Group Inc.

data.title: The Gabelli Value 25 Fund Inc. Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT SCORECARD

The first quarter of 2025 was largely marked by a flight to safety ahead of President Trump's tariff announcements in early April. The largest contributor to Q1 returns was National Fuel Gas (NFG, 5.2% of net assets as of March 31, 2025, +31%), a diversified energy company with E&P, pipeline, and storage, and gas utility businesses centered in Western New York and Pennsylvania that posted excellent results underpinned by firmer natural gas prices and strong execution. Gold prices climbed throughout the quarter, benefiting mining giant Newmont Corp. (NEM, 5.7%, +30%). Finally, the utility-like, inflation-indexed nature of waste disposal companies Republic Services (RSG, 5.7%, +21%) and Waste Connections (WCN, 2.4%,+13%) made them top contributors. Among Q1 detractors were Dolan family-controlled Madison Square Garden Sports (MSGS, 4.6%, -14%), owner of the NY Knicks and Rangers, and Sphere Entertainment (SPHR, -19%), owner of the eponymous Las Vegas venue and Knicks/Rangers television rights holder MSG Networks; the ongoing restructuring of Networks' non-recourse bank debt has proven an overhang on both stocks. Other detractors included global distilled spirits maker Diageo (DEO, 2.0%, -16%), where tariff concerns have compounded an industry-wide slump in consumption, and lodging REIT Ryman Hospitality (RHP, 1.7%, -11%), which has been hit with concerns over future business and leisure travel spending.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

National Fuel Gas Co. (5.2% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (NFG - $79.19 - NYSE), based in Williamsville, New York, is a gas and pipeline utility with a growing exploration and production business. The gas utility serves 754,000 customers in Buffalo, New York, and Erie and Sharon, Pennsylvania. The pipeline & storage (P&S) business operates 3,000 miles of pipe and 34 storage facilities primarily in the state of New York. The E&P business, Seneca Resources, operates in Appalachia (owns 1.2 million net acres), primarily the Marcellus and Utica shales. Seneca's proved gas reserves at year-end FY 2024 were 4,753 Bcfe (compared to 4,536 Bcfe in FY 2023). Forward gas prices have risen due to growing LNG exports and growing electric power demand. NFG is using free cash flow to buyback stock, and improve credit profile and overall financial strength. NFG raised the annual dividend for the 54th consecutive year to $2.06 per share from $1.98 per share.

Republic Services Inc. (5.7%) (RSG - $242.16 - NYSE), based in Phoenix, Arizona, is the second largest solid waste company in North America. Republic provides nonhazardous solid waste collection services for commercial, industrial, municipal, and residential customers in forty-one states and Puerto Rico. Republic serves more than 2,800 municipalities. Republic has benefited from synergies driven by route density, beneficial use of acquired assets, and reduction in redundant corporate overhead. We expect continued solid waste and environmental solutions growth acquisitions, earnings improvement, and incremental route density and internalization growth in already established markets to generate real value in the near to medium term, highlighting the company's potential.

Sony Corp. (8.4%) (SONY - $25.39 - NYSE) is a global conglomerate based in Tokyo, Japan, focusing on direct-to-consumer entertainment products. Sony is the #1 integrated global gaming company with its PlayStation 5 gaming platform and video game development studios. Sony Music Recording commands #2 and Music Publishing #1 global share. Sony Music is capitalizing the growth of streaming and higher music royalty fees. Sony also operates the Sony/Columbia film studio, which is well positioned in the OTT streaming wars as a major supplier of high-quality library shows. Sony is the number one image sensor leader and the sole supplier to Apple iPhones. Sony is planning to spin-off its Sony Financial Services in October 2025. Sony executed a 5-for-1 split in early October 2024.

data.title: QDPL Brings High-Income Appeal And Growth Stock Exposure
data.description:
Pacer Metaurus US Large Cap Dividend Multiplier 400 ETF (NYSEARCA:QDPL) is an exchange-traded fund that aims to provide investors with a cash distribution equal to 400% of the S&P 500 (SPX) ordinary yield while aiming to reduce exposure to SPX performance. The portfolio is benchmarked to the Metaurus US Large Cap Dividend Multiplier Index – Series 400.

QDPL is an enhanced income strategy that aims to provide investors with a cash distribution equivalent to 400% of the S&P 500. This is achieved by structuring the portfolio across equities, treasuries, and dividend futures. Pacer purchases dividend futures, which are essentially the purchase of future S&P 500 dividend distributions, at a discount to analyst expectations. The thesis behind this is that analysts underestimate dividend growth, allowing the portfolio to benefit from the higher-than-expected dividend payout. As a result of the dividend risk premium, the portfolio strategy does not require leverage to achieve its target 400% dividend payout.

Given the structure of the portfolio, distributions are not entirely sourced from dividends. The strategy’s distributions are primarily return of capital with a smaller proportion being capital gains/losses on future dividend contracts and dividends from equities held.

One of the components tied to achieving distributions is equity P/L as part of the portfolio’s equity strategy. The equity component pays out a certain proportion of the dividend cash flow while realizing share price performance. Though the portfolio has certain hedges against the S&P 500 built in, the strategy has over 500 positions, inclusive of cash, treasuries, and futures.

Looking at the top positions, I have reason to believe QDPL’s performance may outperform the S&P 500. QDPL’s top 10 holdings show some resemblance to the S&P 500 (SPY) with the exception of a few tickers, weights, and dividend futures contracts. In total, the top 10 positions account for 39.44% of the total portfolio weight, with Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Apple (AAPL) being the top 3 equity positions. From a high level, I believe both Nvidia and Microsoft have strong growth qualities as data center compute capacity is built out to support demand for AI and non-AI workloads. This thesis also includes Amazon (AMZN) and Broadcom (AVGO).

Accordingly, Broadcom is working with a variety of the hyperscalers in developing custom XPUs, or custom AI accelerator chips purpose-built for their respective data center environments. Though Amazon’s business model is exposed to additional macroeconomic factors relating to international trade tariffs in its retail business, I believe the organization will remain resilient between its cloud services and advertising. You can follow the links tied to the company names to read my most recent investment theses.

Looking at total returns, QDPL performs relatively similar to the S&P 500 with a 222bps deviation when considering the 3-year performance. The outperformance may be attributed to the strategy’s active management and elevated distributions.

When comparing price returns, not inclusive of distributions, the S&P 500 outperformed the portfolio strategy by a wide margin. Nonetheless, assessing the strategy by total returns will be most suitable.

Fund Details

QDPL was founded in 2021, performing just under its benchmark across all timeframes. Comparing QDPL to the SPX, QDPL provided significantly less downside risk over the provided time periods. This is the result of the portfolio minimizing the exposure of equity risk while maximizing distribution exposure.

QDPL charges investors a 60bps expense ratio, a relatively premium to comparable dividend-yielding ETFs.

Despite charging a higher fee, QDPL has outperformed its peers by a wide margin when comparing historical total returns. Though future performance is not guaranteed, I believe the structure of QDPL can provide investors with a durable income vehicle while reducing equity exposure relative to the SPX.

QDPL pays out a quarterly distribution. As referenced above, QDPL utilizes dividend futures to provide investors with an above-index distribution, targeting 400%. For the quarter ending March 31, 2025, QDPL paid out $0.5272/share, equating to a forward yield of 5.46%. For comparison purposes, the trailing twelve-month distribution summed to $2.1033/share, yielding roughly 5.45%. This compares to SPY paying out $7.17/share with a yield of 1.20%.

Risks Related to Holding this Strategy

Despite the futures positions that may act as a hedge for QDPL, the portfolio does come with certain risks to be aware of. QDPL is highly correlated with the S&P 500, resembling an R-squared of 97%.

This may result in certain adverse performance in the event of a market correction, despite exposure to the index being 88%. QDPL is also an actively managed, rules-based strategy, meaning that performance is dependent on the strategy set by the portfolio manager team at Pacer. Given the dividend-yielding components to the strategy, QDPL may be exposed to a certain degree of tracking error. Lastly, the distributions are spread across ordinary income, long-term/short-term capital gains, and return of capital, potentially exposing the investor to certain tax implications; I recommend consulting with an advisor prior to making an investment decision.

Conclusion

QDPL poses a certain appeal as an income/growth portfolio with 88% exposure to the S&P 500. Given the appealing distribution yield and price appreciation component, I believe QDPL may pose a strong candidate for an income-generating strategy for a long-term investor’s portfolio. With the rationale that QDPL is constructed to provide investors with a broad equity exposure across a broad range of industries, I believe QDPL has the potential to act as an investor’s equity allocation for those seeking a higher income-generating strategy. As a result, I am recommending QDPL with a BUY rating.

data.title: The Gabelli Equity Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT SCORECARD

There were many new initiatives from the Trump administration in the first quarter of 2025, but the biggest market moving initiative seemed to be tariffs. President Trump had spoken about tariffs often on the campaign trail and about his desire to impose higher tariffs on our trading partners to improve trade imbalances as well as build up the manufacturing base of America. During the first quarter, however, there were no specific details for what a new tariff program might look like. At the end of the quarter, President Trump did say he would make an announcement on April 2, 2025, concerning his program for new higher tariffs. He called April 2 "Liberation Day" for America. On that day, however, he outlined tariffs that were far higher than the stock market was expecting, and the market moved down in a big way. The second quarter will clearly see lots of negotiations with trading partners about reciprocal tariffs, and stock market volatility will stay with us in the short term.

In the first quarter, the total return of the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) was down about 4%, with value stocks, as measured by the S&P/Citigroup Value Index, about flat, and growth stocks, as measured by the S&P/Citigroup Growth Index, down over 8%. Long-term interest rates, as measured by the 10-year U.S. Treasury Note (US10Y), decreased by about 40 basis points during the quarter, from 4.6% to 4.2%. The Fed kept short-term interest rates steady at 4.5%. Although the Fed did not make any moves in the first quarter, we expect the Fed will lower rates this year, but at a slow and gradual pace. Although inflation has been coming down, the tariff situation makes predicting inflation over the next year very difficult.

Of the eleven sectors that make up the S&P 500 Index, most were actually up in the first quarter, with only four sectors down. The best performing sector in the quarter was Energy, up 10%, followed by Health Care, which was up 6%.

The worst performing sector was Discretionary, down about 14%, with Technology down about 13%.

One of the best performing stocks in (y)our portfolio was National Fuel Gas (NFG, 2.0% of net assets as of March 31, 2024), a gas utility company in New York that also owns substantial natural gas resources in Pennsylvania. Other top contributors to performance in the quarter were CVS (1.1%), the pharmacy group, and Newmont Mining (NEM, 1.5%), the gold company. One of the top detractors in (y)our portfolio was Diageo (DEO, 1.2%), the global spirits company, which would be negatively affected by higher tariffs. Other top detractors were technology company Microsoft (MSFT, 2.0%) and Brown-Forman (BF.A, BF.B, 1.4%), the maker of Jack Daniels bourbon.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*

Bank Of New York Mellon Corp.(BK) 4.9%
Genuine Parts Co. (GPC) 3.8
GATX Corp. (GATX) 3.0
Deere & Co. (DE) 2.8
State Street Corp. (STT) 2.3
National Fuel Gas Co. (NFG) 2.0
Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) 2.0
Mueller Industries Inc. (MLI) 2.0
Crane Co. (CR) 2.0
Hess Corp. (HES) 1.7
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Bank of New York Mellon Corp. (4.9% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (BK - $83.87 - NYSE) is a global leader in providing financial services to institutions and individuals. The company operates in more than one hundred markets worldwide and strives to be the global provider of choice for investment management and investment services. As of March 31, the firm had $53.1 trillion in assets under custody and $2.0 trillion in assets under management. Going forward, BK is benefiting from higher interest rates, rising global incomes, and cross-border financial transactions.

Deere & Co. (2.8%) (DE - $469.35 - NYSE), headquartered in Moline, Illinois, is a leading global manufacturer of machinery for agricultural, construction, and forestry use. Its dominant position in North American agricultural equipment markets optimally positions the company for what is expected to be an increase in demand for agricultural equipment both in the near term, given cycle dynamics, as well as in the long term, as global population and income growth drive crop demand in the coming decades. Its premium product portfolio and strong balance sheet position it well to thrive as its end markets recover. Moreover, DE is a leader in "Precision Ag" technologies that improve farmer productivity through cloud and Al-based improvements on centuries-old farming techniques.

GATX Corp. (3.0%) (GATX - $155.27 - NASDAQ) leases, manages, and remarkets assets in the rail and spare engine leasing markets in North American and internationally. North American railcar leasing is its largest business, along with a moderately sized European leasing business and a growing presence in India. Engine leasing, which has benefited from the recovery of international travel and delays in new widebody production, includes a 50/50 spare engine leasing JV with Rolls Royce and directly owned spare engines. GATX is the best-in-class railcar lessor. It has an industry high NA leasing utilization of 99% and leading renewal success rates, a function of its railcar mix, strong commercial organization, and maintenance capabilities. While lease rates have plateaued at healthy levels, fleet values continue to increase, given an older stock of railcars and a tighter market following years of heavy scrapping. Tariffs may limit or lift costs of new railcar production and higher steel prices may further lift secondary market values. Meanwhile, international rail and engine leasing should remain growth drivers.

National Fuel Gas Co. (2.0%) (NFG - $79.19 - NYSE), based in Williamsville, New York, is a gas and pipeline utility with a growing exploration and production business. The gas utility serves 754,000 customers in Buffalo, New York and Erie and Sharon, Pennsylvania. The pipeline & storage (P&S) business operates 3,000 miles of pipe and 34 storage facilities primarily in the state of New York. The E&P business, Seneca Resources, operates in Appalachia (owns 1.2 million net acres), primarily the Marcellus and Utica shales. Seneca's proved gas reserves at year-end FY 2024 were 4,753 Bcfe (compared to 4,536 Bcfe in FY 2023). Forward gas prices have risen due to growing LNG exports and growing electric power demand. NFG is using free cash flow to buyback stock and improve credit profile and overall financial strength. NFG raised the annual dividend for the 54th consecutive year to $2.06 per share from $1.98 per share.

data.title: Impax Large Cap Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

  • The fund underperformed the benchmark during the last quarter.

Market overview

  • US large cap equities, as measured by the S&P 500, declined in Q1 after early strength turned negative as uncertainty surrounding US tariffs dominated sentiment and news flow. The continued broadening of markets drove a change in sector-level leadership with technology stocks lagging all sectors during the period. Incoming US President Donald Trump spooked markets with threats, retractions and the eventual implementation of tariffs on key trading partners. Amid the uncertainty, the US Federal Reserve held interest rates in both February and March and projected slower growth and higher core inflation by year-end.
  • From a sector perspective, Energy was at the forefront of index returns as oil prices rallied. Utilities and other defensive sectors also showed relative strength during the period. The pullback in technology continued as the sector delivered the weakest returns of the quarter followed by Consumer Discretionary stocks.
  • Looking beyond the short-term volatility triggered by Trump's tariffs, we believe growth remains on track to improve in 2026. As long-term investors, we aim to manage portfolios through this challenging period with near-term caution and optimism for the longer term.
Performance(%) Total return Average annual return
1M 3M YTD 1Y 3Y 5Y SI1
Institutional Class -6.72 -6.57 -6.57 -3.1 2.98 15.31 12.25
Investor Class -6.75 -6.68 -6.68 -3.36 2.72 15.03 11.98
S&P 500 -5.63 -4.27 -4.27 8.25 9.06 18.59 13.72

Performance data quoted represent past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For most recent month-end performance information, visit Impax Asset Management - Specialists in the Transition to a more Sustainable Economy.

Figures include reinvested dividends, capital gains distribution, and changes in principal value.

1First full month since inception. The inception date for Impax Large Cap Fund Institutional Class is 12/16/2016 and Investor Class is 12/16/2016. As of 05/01/2024 prospectus, total annual Impax Large Cap Fund operating expenses, gross of any fee waivers or reimbursements (excluding Acquired Fund fees and expenses), for Institutional Class and Investor Class shares are 0.73%, 0.98%, respectively.

Key performance drivers

  • The Large Cap fund underperformed in the quarter as markets repriced risk amid the heightened uncertainty around the impacts of an‘America-first' approach to US economic policy. The underperformance was attributed to weak stock selection while sector allocation effects were moderately positive. Stock selection was weakest in Information Technology with Al names Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) and Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) - which had been strong performers over the last year - both selling off as investors raised concerns about how the “DeepSeek” moment and a tariff-related slowdown could impact the pace of data center build outs and Al spend. Communication Services were also weak as Alphabet saw lower-than-expected revenue and cloud growth, while The Walt Disney Company (DIS) sold off on the risk of recession hitting consumer spend on park and cruise experiences and streaming. Health Care was another area of weakness. Align, maker of clear teeth aligners, which the investment team exited, is sensitive to consumer spending and sold off as recession fears heightened. Medical tools names Agilent Technologies, Inc. (A) and Avantor, Inc. (AVTR) were weaker on concerns around the biopharma cycle with the appointment of Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. to lead Human Health Services, as well as the complexity of supply chains in the face of tariffs. Offsetting some of this was strong stock selection in Consumer Discretionary, led by Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) which saw same-store sales come in ahead of estimates as investors closely monitored the turnaround under new CEO Brian Niccol. Real Estate was another area of positive stock selection as senior housing REIT Ventas, Inc. (VTR) had its best year in a decade, beating expectations in the quarter with accelerating organic growth in senior housing set to continue. Warehouse REIT Prologis, Inc. (PLD) was also additive as the company's full-year occupancy guide was substantially ahead of expectations. The portfolio's position in American Water Works Company, Inc. (AWK), performed strongly given its defensive business model as market risk ticked up in the latter half of the quarter.

Contributors

  • Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) (Consumer Discretionary) which is not held in the portfolio was among the worst-performing stocks in the S&P 500 in the quarter amid a decline in deliveries driven by a combination of retooled lines for the refreshed Model Y, backlash for Elon Musk's non-Tesla related activities, and tariff-related risks.
  • Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) (Information Technology) which is not held in the portfolio was weak despite strong earnings. The declines were driven by near-term caution among investors as sentiment affected tech stocks in the wake of the DeepSeek news, and ongoing uncertainty around the firm's VMware acquisition.
  • CVS Health Corporation (CVS) (Health Care) rose as Q4 earnings were better-than-expected with a strong earnings beat.The company also provided attractive guidance for 2025 as Medicare Advantage margins are expected to recover with improved pricing.The company's defensive profile allowed it to hold up well as macro-related volatility increased.

Detractors

  • Marvell Technology (MRVL) (Information Technology) was weaker in the quarter as the combination of DeepSeek and market risk aversion drove the stock lower. The fundamentals remain in place, and the order book remains stable despite market sentiment.
  • Alphabet Inc. Class C (GOOG) (GOOGL) (Communication Services) sold off with other tech-related names over the quarter. In addition, the company saw disappointing results as revenue and cloud growth did not meet investor expectations. The company announced plans for US$75bn in capex, 25% higher than previous expectations, which was also a cause for concern among investors.
  • ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) (Information Technology) was down despite solid results as investors had more elevated expectations as the stock ran into earnings. Top and bottom lines both met expectations with current remaining performance obligations (cRPOs) coming in a bit weaker than guidance suggested. The firm continues to grow its client base and, through its partnership with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), offers a compelling value proposition for businesses looking to drive Al-related efficiency gains.

Outlook

  • The macro environment remains complex with announcements on tariffs triggering justifiable concerns over higher inflation, potential consumer weakness and business uncertainty.The investment team in actively monitoring the portfolio, considering both the direct and indirect impacts, such as supply chain dislocations, inflation, and a potential weaker macroeconomic global environment. Despite the tariff implications, innovation in technology and health care, coupled with ongoing developments in housing and infrastructure, are areas of the market the investment team believe are attractively positioned to benefit from secular growth opportunities.
  • The investment team has strategically repositioned the portfolio, taking advantage of the market volatility to focus on long-term secular winners with durable competitive advantages, trading at significant discounts to intrinsic value. Valuations across the portfolio are currently attractive relative to fundamental expectations, positioning the portfolio well as market conditions stabilize.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Gabelli Asset Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT SCORECARD

Top contributors during the first quarter included SONY (3.1% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (+20%), which reported strong growth of 16% in its Game & Networks Services business and 14% in its Music business. Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B, 2.6%, +17%) shares rose as markets slid, as Chairman Warren Buffett has amassed a $334 billion cash pile which may be deployed into public equities or entire companies at more advantageous prices. Waste collection leader Republic Services (RSG, 2.3%, +21%) gained as the company's recurring revenue and pricing power position it to be able to withstand an economic slowdown and tariffs. Shares of Newmont Mining (NEM, 1.1%, +30%) increased due to the price of gold rising to all-time highs during the quarter. Finally, shares of Deere (DE, 2.4%, +11%) rose on expectations that excess channel inventory had been sufficiently removed to align with softer agricultural equipment demand, setting the stage for a strong recovery when broader conditions for the farmer improve.

Detractors included distilled spirits producers Diageo (DEO, 1.2%, -16%) and Brown-Forman (BF.A, BF.B, 1.2%, -11%), which are contending with a prolonged slowdown in the U.S. spirits market and could be caught up in the tariff war globally (though both have largely remained unscathed so far). Knicks' and Rangers' owner Madison Square Garden Sports (MSGS, 1.5%, -14%) declined as the local media rights environment evolves. Flowserve (FLS, 1.1%, -15%) shares declined on a weaker than expected earnings result and outlook, coupled by a decline in the price of oil, an important end market for the company. Caterpillar (CAT, 1.9%, -9%) shares declined on global macroeconomic concerns regarding a broader slowdown that would impact the purchase and utilization of capital equipment in areas such as construction, energy, and transportation.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

When discussing specific stocks in the portfolios of the Funds, favorable earnings prospects do not necessarily translate into higher stock prices, but they do express a positive trend that we believe will develop over time. Individual securities mentioned are not necessarily representative of a Fund's entire portfolio. For the holdings discussed, the percentage of the Fund's net assets and their share prices stated in U.S. dollar equivalent terms are presented as of March 31, 2025.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*

Ametek Inc. (AME) 3.1%
Sony Group Corp. (SONY) 3.1
Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A)(BRK.B) 2.6
Deere & Co. (DE) 2.4
Republic Services Inc. (RSG) 2.3
Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) 1.9
Bank Of New York Mellon Corp. (BK) 1.8
Mastercard Inc. (MA) 1.7
American Express Co. (AXP) 1.5
Madison Square Garden Sports Co. (MSGS) 1.5
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

Baxter International Inc. (0.2% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (ΒΑΧ - $34.23 - NYSE) is one of the largest manufacturers of hospital products in the world, which includes infusion pumps, beds, and injectable pharmaceuticals. The company recently sold its legacy kidney care division to the Carlyle Group for $3.25 billion after-tax proceeds, which it used to pay down debt. With the completion of the sale, CEO Joe Almeida retired, which we view as positive given his mixed track record. The new Baxter has the opportunity for faster revenue growth and significant margin improvement as it launches a new generation of infusion pumps and connectivity solutions for hospitals. The company's local manufacturing strategy should help mitigate global tariffs while the company continues to benefit from elevated healthcare utilization rates in the United States.

Crane Co. (1.5%) (CR - $153.18 - NYSE) based in Stamford, Connecticut, is a diversified manufacturer of highly engineered industrial products comprised of two business segments: Aerospace & Electronics and Process Flow Technologies. In April 2023 the company separated into two independent companies, in which the Payment and Merchandising Technologies business became "Crane NXT" and the Aerospace & Electronics and Process Flow Technologies business retained the Crane Co. name. Crane's long term vision is to build two strategic growth platforms with Aerospace & Electronics and Process Flow Technologies, focusing on building both of those businesses to $2 billion each in revenue with 20%+ adjusted EBITDA margins by 2028.

Donaldson Company (1.2%) (DCI - $67.06 - NYSE) based in Minneapolis, Minnesota, is a global manufacturer of worldwide filtration systems and replacement parts. The company's products include air and liquid filtration systems and exhaust and emission control products. Further, Donaldson has spoken to the opportunities ahead with its life sciences vertical integration, in which the company has completed five acquisitions over the last five years. This includes its most recent acquisition of 49% of MEDICA, a company that makes medical filters for kidney dialysis.

Herc Holdings Inc. (0.3%) (HRI - $134.27 - NYSE), based in Bonita Springs, Florida, is the third largest equipment rental company in the United States. HRI was spun out of former parent Hertz on June 30, 2016. Underemphasized as part of a significantly larger car rental company, HRI has worked for the past three years to put its operating metrics in line with larger, better known peers such as United Rentals. The company is well positioned to generate considerable cash as the U.S. equipment rental market grows over the next several years. Additionally, management efforts to improve profitability metrics to more closely align with its larger peers could result in valuation multiple expansion. The company's recent announcement that it would be acquiring H&E Equipment Services in a cash and stock deal should enable the company to continue to expand its specialty rental offerings in fast growing U.S. markets.

PORTFOLIO HIGHLIGHTS
Total Net Assets: $1.5 Billion
NAV (Class I): $45.80
Turnover: (A) 5%
Inception Date: 03/03/86
Expense Ratio: (B) 1.13%
(A) For the twelve months ended December 31, 2024. (B) As of the current prospectus dated April 29, 2024.
SHARE CLASS (C) SYMBOL
Class AAA: GABAX
Class A: GATAX
Class I: GABIX
(C) Another class of shares is available.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS: Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A)

Gabelli Asset Fund QTR 1 Year 5 Year 10 Year 15 Year Since Inception (03/03/86)
Class I (MUTF:GABIX) (B) 1.28% 2.91% 13.95% 7.55% 9.50% 11.13%
S&P 500 Index (C) (4.27) 8.25 18.59 12.50 13.15 10.94

Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

(A) Other classes of shares are available, with different characteristics. For additional information please contact your financial advisor or call 800-GABELLI.

(B) Returns would have been lower had Gabelli Funds, LLC (the Adviser) not reimbursed certain expenses of the Fund for periods prior to December 31, 1988. The Class AAA Share NAVs are used to calculate performance for the period prior to the issuance of Class I Shares on January 11, 2008. The actual performance of the Class I Shares would have been higher due to lower expenses related to this class of shares. The Fund imposes a 2% redemption fee on shares sold or exchanged within seven days of purchase.

(C) The S&P 500 Index is a market capitalization weighted index of 500 large capitalization stocks commonly used to represent the U.S. equity market. Dividends are considered reinvested. You cannot invest directly in an index. S&P 500 Index since inception performance is as of February 28, 1986.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate. When shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Visit Home for performance information as of the most recent month end.

The Asset Fund is subject to the risk that the portfolio securities' PMV may never be realized by the market, or that the portfolio securities' prices decline.

Kevin V. Dreyer, Christopher J. Marangi, Mario J. Gabelli, CFA, Alec Boccanfuso, Melody P. Bryant, Sarah Donnelly, Jeffrey J. Jonas, CFA, Brian C. Sponheimer, Ashish Sinha, Hendi Sustano, Simon Wong
